title: 2025/08/21 - FD (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-08-21 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - Briefing on the Ministry of Defence Major Projects Report 2024 [Part 1]
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: Briefing on the Ministry of Defence Major Projects Report 2024
description: Thursday, 21 August 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee Briefing on the Ministry of Defence Major Projects Report 2024 Hearing of evidence Submitters list: Ministry of Defence and New Zealand Defence Force Brigadier Matthew Weston, Assistant Chief Capability - New Zealand Defence Force Sarah Minson, Deputy Secretary Capability Delivery - Ministry of Defence Briefing on non-tariff barriers Orion Health Andrew Bowater, Vice President - Operations
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the Ministry of Defence Major Projects Report 2024. Ministry of Defence and New Zealand Defence Force. Brigadier Matthew Weston, Assistant Chief Capability - New Zealand Defence Force

chunks.text: All right. Good morning. Morning. Morning, Everyone. Welcome along to the committee. Today, we are looking forward to today's hearing on the defense Major Projects report for 2024. Um, we have half an hour sit down for this morning's discussion. I'm sure there'll be plenty of questions from members, but we'd like to hand over to you both for initial comments and then we can delve into it. Thank you.

Thank you, um, for the opportunity to attend this oral hearing today for the defense Major projects Report 2024. Uh, my name is Sarah Minson. I'm the Defense Secretary Capability Delivery at the Ministry of Defense. And with me today is the Defense Force Assistant Chief Capability, Bri Matt. We, this is the 15th edition of the Major Projects Report.

The series was established to provide greater visibility and scrutiny of major defense acquisition projects. And in the report you will see that we are reporting on projects across four domains. So there's 12 projects across four domains. Maritime, land, air, and information delivering new or upgraded capability remains vital to ensuring that defense can continue to deliver safe and effective safely and effectively to the level that the government requires, and that our allies and partners have come to expect.

The ongoing publication of this report demonstrates a commitment to transparency and assurance about defense managed projects. I'd like to acknowledge the work of Audit New Zealand, who have provided independent, uh, reviews of these reports since 2019. The projects in this report are part of an overall portfolio of around 6 billion worth of ministry led capability projects.

And this report focuses on the medium to high risk projects that we are delivering. It includes $2.4 billion acquisition of the P eight Poseidon fleet and the associated training materials and systems and infrastructure, as well as the 1.5 billion replacement of the Hercules fleet and associated infrastructure and training systems.

Both of those large projects were delivered ahead of schedule and under budget. And last week we were proud to celebrate the formal opening of the home of the p eights and number five squadron. At IP alongside current projects, we are focused on what is required to deliver projects within the 2025 defense capability plan. The plan commitments of 12 billion over the next four years is significant.

We must deliver on time within budget and within scope, providing value for money, but also providing innovative thinking. The operating model we use to deliver project delivery. The cap, the Defense Capability Management system is a proven model that is scalable and resilient. Clearly, our industry partners are a key part of successful delivery, and we look forward to reporting in future on the defense industry strategy that is in development and will be released in the next few months.

I'll now pass on to Brigette Western for smoking comments. Koto, it's a pleasure to be here with you today to discuss the, uh, major defense capability projects. The world remains an unstable and dangerous place, one where the rules based international order that we rely on New Zealand is constantly under threat. This topic has been well covered in previous committees, so I won't dwell on it.

The major projects and indeed the Defense Capability Plan are designed to improve the New Zealand Defense Force's ability to understand partner and then act in response to the New Zealand security needs. We in the N-Z-N-Z-D-F understand investments and military capability often come at the cost of other priorities. We are well aware that it was our responsibility to turn major projects into productive military capabilities that serve New Zealand's needs.

This requires the integration of input such as infrastructure, workforce, information systems, and data, and key aspects of interop interoperability such as training and doctrine. In doing so, we must continue to be rigorous in managing cost pressures and budget. Due to the current global security situation, there is an imperative to deliver military capabilities whilst continuing to improve the New Zealand Defense Forces Preparedness and Readiness to operate.

Now, the NZD has maintaining its commitment to the rules based international order for ongoing operations to support the Ukraine in the Middle East and in North Asia. The, at the same time, we are fielding new capabilities as we improve our interoperability and demonstrate intent to act within the Pacific to both our partners and to potential malign actors.

Recent examples include Thar's current deployment to the Middle East and the Indo-Pacific, and she returns with her crew this Sunday after seven months, deployed a significant deployment to excise Talisman Saber, which include new network enabled Army capabilities and our newly acquired Bushmaster vehicles.

The deployment of UNC crewed maritime systems to provide maritime domain awareness in Fiji, and that is ongoing as we speak. And the recent use of p eights in North Asia and the southwest Pacific for maritime domain awareness and our new C one 30 js for meeting operations in the Antarctica. That was well covered then.

NZDF is operating at a high tempo, has work to understand partner and then act while, while still say developing and delivering new capabilities guided by the defense capability plan. Our people are making great progress, building their combat capability and a avoid by the government's commitment to invest in their ability to defend New Zealand and our interests.

We continue to operate with courage, commitment, comradeship, and integrity. Thank you very much. Excellent. Thank you both for those introductory comments. Um, happy to take questions from members just to confirm we are in our live session, uh, Tim Costley. Thanks, Mr. Chair. Um, thanks for joining us. I do, We have the agility that we need to bring in emerging technology to upgrade systems.

And I'm looking at, you know, when we spend 44 months in the capability definition phase for frigate sustainment, uh, when we spent 55 months in capability definition for protected mobility, The next War isn't gonna wait 45, 55 months for us to decide, um, just to get to that stage.

And, and, and maybe we can talk about some of the timelines on projects like, um, network enabled Army in a minute. But, but firstly, just add a big picture. Have we got the settings right? Is is the big picture where it needs to be to, to bring capabilities to New Zealand? Yeah, I can, um, I'll make some comments and I'll hand over to, um, Bridy Western. Um, we have been focusing very hard on our, um, on those processes that you refer to, to get the capability into service faster.

We've been working with treasury to make sure that we understand and can refine the better business case process. And as an example of where we have, um, proven that we can do things faster is with the recent 7, 5 7, uh, procurement process. We put an indicative business case up for government last September, a detailed business case in December, and a, uh, implementation business case, um, in the middle of this year.

So we, we can prove that we can go faster. Um, so I think we do have the agility that we need. The capability management system is, um, flexible and we are continually making improvements to that so that we can go faster. Um, so I'll hand over to Matt, but what I take your point, Tim, but we are definitely focused on doing things a lot faster.

Yeah, I mean, I, I think it's a very salient question. Um, you know, the emergence of new threats is, you know, is happening quickly mm-hmm. And faster than, um, often traditional capability development has occurred. Um, and it's a challenge all of our partners face. Um, what we are experiencing is, um, there is a lot of pressure on the, um, supply chain and, um, industry to d to D, you know, deploy new, um, and existing capabilities and getting into the queues really important where we are actually experiencing delays

where we've done all the work we need to do, but then we've got weight to the production line can produce, um, elements for us. Um, in terms of high tech, um, capabilities, we, we are looking at new ways of operating and within the defense capability plan there is the, uh, tech accelerator, which is a, um, we are developing the operating model for that now, which will be a focused on New Zealand, um, high tech industry and how we can take dual use or new, um, initiatives and field them quickly within the military or help their company, uh, prepare it for, um,

use in another military and for export. Um, we are experimenting with new technologies as we speak in the, um, UNC crewed systems we're using in Fiji at the moment have come to, into use very quickly. Um, they haven't been through the full capability development cycle, but we are using them to affect now.

Yeah, and I guess that's, that, that's maybe a good example. Like I hear reports that in Ukraine, unit commanders are empowered to make capability decisions, acquire, uh, you know, that sort of unmanned systems. Yeah, it's not a new frigate. Um, but they need that agility. And if we look at, at every conflict through history about think of World War ii, the, the rate of change and the speed that you need to, to introduce capability, are we stuck in a, uh, a peace time mentality where there is time for that wheel to, to turn the,

I think there's a real, I mean, the Ukraine example is an interesting one, and it's a real challenge of course, when you are, when you've got something more than an existential threat and, you know, natural mobilization, you, you do move faster. Um, as we bring in new, uh, equipments and technologies that we still have a absolute responsibility around training and safety and assurance and, um, and how we spend government money.

So we have to be very sure that we are borrowing the right things at the right time, which does tend to move the delegations for experimentation higher within the organization, I think, which is fair. Um, we are working with our partners and allies on how we can do things quicker, um, and how we share, um, technological advancements, experimentation, uh, together so we are not replicating other people.

And, and just if I can a final one on that and, and I take the point you made about the 7, 5 7 as a recent project is a good example of moving a lot quicker. What about in terms of the implementation phase? And, and, and I could look through a range of projects from, but if I look at Network enabled Army, um, which started in 2015 and has had a co been rebaselined at least twice between 2019 and 2023, it looks like in that period, significant delays.

Um, and, and because this report's a year old, it's a bit hard to tell, but, um, it seems like a very long implementation timeframe and we are not even at full operational release yet. And the same for protected mobility. So is there an update on that or are we moving with urgency to get them fully operational? Um, Yeah, I'll make a general comment then maybe Matt can talk more specifically about the complexity of what NEA is trying to do.

Um, we are very focused on getting, as soon as we've got the capability, getting it introduced as quickly as possible. With the recent, um, purchases of the C one thirties and the P eights, we had those aircraft introduced into service and fully used by the NCDF, um, very quickly within months of the aircraft arriving for, um, to be able to be used.

So we are focused, um, on that, um, protective mobility. So the Bush Masters, we've received them, um, we've put comms into them and they were deployed recently into T and Saber. So while there is a, um, as I'm sure you're aware, there is a project to put, um, more high tech comms into those vehicles.

They are being used in an operational sense already. So we are trying to phase the release of operation of these capabilities so that, that we can get them out into the hands of the war fighter as soon as possible while we finish off the remainder of the, um, trading and checks and balances. But NEA is, um, I'll get Matt to talk a little bit more about NEA 'cause it's not just like by the ship.

Yeah. Um, there's a number of aspects I'd like to highlight with the network enabled Army. Um, one is that, uh, MOD and NZD did make some prioritization decisions, um, some years ago for good reason, which meant that NEA wasn't resourced as quickly as it might have been, um, as we've tried to, um, NEA as a, you know, delivers a mesh to network to the army of communications, command and control and, um, reconnaissance and surveillance, um, assets, um, as it's been, uh, I guess slowed down a little.

Um, the situation's changed. We've had to, we've gone from a emphasis on quite static command and control, so, you know, a command post that sat on the ground. Um, now looking at the Ukraine example, if you emit and don't move, if you die. So, um, you know, we've had to go to more mobile command and control, uh, situations.

At the same time the technologies have changed, so we've had to adapt. Um, we're at, we're in a good place to move forward now. We've proven the network works. Um, our special forces are fully equipped with network enabled Army capability, um, and our most deployable, uh, conventional land forces are equipped with it as well.

Uh, on Taliman Sabre, we utilized the, um, the drones for surveillance. They were very successful and, um, we had good data connectivity. Um, it's interesting, the Australian army was in front of us on this journey and they've had to change technologies and now they've basically dropped behind. Um, the next, uh, intended next step with network enabled army is now to populate the army with the radios they require.

So it goes across the force at the moment. We've got small parts of the force using it, and I think that will be a step change for the army. Okay. Supplementary Financiary Walters, Thank you. Thank you. And thank you for being with us today and for the work of the broader team in terms of interoperability and artificial intelligence.

I'm just curious about, um, what the current approach is in terms of the development of autonomous technology, um, just given that, it appears to me that there are quite different standards being, but there's at least no agreement internationally in terms of what the appropriate ethical criteria are. So, um, we need to develop and confirm New Zealand's policy around au autonomous, um, technologies.

Um, obviously, um, some AI tools are perfectly, um, you know, I'll use the word benign, you know, running your organization, but there is potential to use AI in a, in a manner that, uh, I'm not sure where to ethically but be comfortable with. So there's a piece of work around that. Um, some of the coming upgrades that we are planning for, um, some of our systems would utilize AI for, um, sorting intelligence, uh, and information making sense of a large volume, um, and then creating, uh, intelligence that we can use quickly.

And I think, um, that along with how we understand our supply chain, um, and other aspects are definitely things we need to move forward into. There are some issues we need to, um, solve around, um, security of our information networks and how the AI works from that. Um, but we are, I mean, I had a meeting with a, uh, medium sized New Zealand firm earlier this week around AI tools that we could use to assist in capability development.

Um, so yeah, it's a broad subject. Lots of use. Yeah. Thanks. Um, so just talking about NEA and the delivery, so obviously it's been very long time. Um, I understand there's quite a few complexities with that project, but given, um, the way in which tech is moving so quickly now quicker than it was in 2015, um, what, like mistakes or what have you learned from the start of this project to what you need to do now to give the public confidence that you will actually be able to deliver?

Um, is any NEA still fit for purpose or do you need to actually look at the landscape now and maybe make some changes? Um, I, I would contend that NEA is absolutely fit for purpose now. I think, um, there is a standard of, uh, security around your military communications that you must meet in a five eyes environment.

NEA is absolutely, um, of that standard and, uh, better than a lot of of people are utilizing. Mm-hmm. Um, the systems are absolutely upgradable, you know, whilst you have a green box, it's a software inside it that you can adjust. Um, and, you know, the effect of NEA will make our soldiers a lot safer and a lot more effective on the battlefield side.

And I is, um, it's able to be adapted. Mm-hmm. What I would say is, um, you know, the traditional, you know, you buy a big steel thing and have it for 20 years and upgrade it once that NEA will be continually upgraded as we go, as will all of our information and, um, and communication systems going forward.

The only thing I'd add to that, um, just to reinforce what Matt said, is the underlying, um, battle management system that we use as a system, um, called by a company called Systematic, which is providing to NATO countries. Mm-hmm. So they are clearly constantly updating their software, and we are part of that group mm-hmm. Tiano to Be on. Yeah. Uh, thank you for, um, thank you for your, um, presentation so far.

I I, I was just thinking in the context of, of threats of, of the ability for these projects to respond to environmental threats. Yeah. Uh, we are, you know, living in a climate change world when we travel around the Pacific, this is one of the things they always talk to us about, ex existential threat of, of climate change. Um, and when we do look at generally at, at threats that are, uh, more conflict orientated, you can kind of see them bubbling away, uh, for, for a while, but sometimes these things just happen all of a sudden.

And we are looking at kind of long term, um, kind of midterm projects like for example, moving over to Australia and pathway for them. How are, how easy, uh, will these projects respond to, like disaster relief, humanitarian aid, uh, with a particular focus on the Pacific Start, Sheila? Yeah.

So, um, I think, you know, the projects that very clearly have, um, high utility, um, the C one 30 J, um, you know, that delivers an aircraft with more load capacity, um, than the, than its predecessor. Um, and, you know, great ability to get into tight and difficult airfields, um, the Maritime Fleet Review will, um, which is projected, but we are looking at, you know, how do we project into the Pacific better, both in terms of, um, gaining access to information quickly.

So, um, uh, so better situational understanding, understanding is a disaster unfolds, but then how do we get, uh, New Zealand assets there quickly? Um, and that's just not military assets, but aid. Um, so, you know, keeping the Canterbury, the vessel Canterbury running until we replace it perhaps in the mid thirties is gonna be very important.

Um, you know, commander control systems will be very important in disasters because obviously, you know, your civilian, uh, networks will be likely to be impaired. Um, so our ability to get into the Pacific with aid and help, I think will be, and then understand what's going on, will be the key, these Projects. And, and just to that agility point, um, and talking earlier about the NDAs, uh, and technology does move really, really quickly, um, have you factored that in, in terms of movement of technology?

So if something happens, you can kind of switch it up a little Bit. Most of our large platforms in the, in the defense capability plan, looking forward, our large platforms will have upgrades provisioned for, so we can, a lot of these, um, systems, you know, we we're almost buying into a club, so, you know, you upgrade is like your, I find upgrades, we'll upgrade our systems as, as that technology develops, and we do it together with our partners.

And, and just one, one last thing around interoperability, um, and, and I get the point around ensuring that we are interop, so you can plug and move quickly, but is there a point where, um, where you can become un, un inoperable if the tired of what partners are doing is not in line with what we want to do?

So, sorry. Yeah, no, you go. That's fine. Um, sovereignty is key. So yeah, we, you don't, um, we don't buy into interoperability and lose sovereign decision making. Um, that's really important, you know, with new technologies so that we, you know, that we are using technologies that we are ethically comfortable with and that our, our government is comfortable with.

But also in terms of, you know, some, um, interoperability efforts with our partners are just so expensive, we can't afford to go there. So we need to make really clear on decisions. Interoperability, um, is important and essential, but where it adds value and it shouldn't add, there's a bit of burden to maintain, uh, interoperable systems, but it shouldn't, um, you know, overburden us to the point where we, you know, can't make good self decisions.

Thank you. Finish your als. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, my question is around the ability of New Zealand to be defense ready and the context of the job cuts that have happened, but also training. So I understand at the end of July, it was confirmed that 281 civilian staff roles would be disestablished, but there was a piece in the post this morning as well talking about the fact that the canceling of exercises reducing Air Force flying hours and Navy days at sea, slowing the recruitment of new personnel and taking voluntary redundancies point

of hasn't been sufficient to close the deficit. So it's a question, not just, Sorry. Um, Mr. Chair, I just wonder if we can stick to the, um, major reports that we've got. We, We, it's about capacity to Exactly, sorry, point, points of water hearing in silence. Um, we are varying into areas that, that we would need the secretary and the chief of defense to answer.

If it's about, um, the, the personnel aspect of capability. This is about delivering projects, Speaking to the point of order, they did bring out the issues of workforce in the introduction, but I do have What said as relevant and it's key to being able to deliver the key projects. So Yeah, so the key thrust of today's briefing is around defense major projects.

So yes, in the context of that, you can ask the question potentially around some of the other Mr. Chair, they must link to the major Projects. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Uh, so in the context of delivering the major projects, um, my question is around whether there's sufficient capacity as well as sufficient training, or whether you are seeing that you are having to pull that back and potentially forward looking, having to pull that back in a way that would be detrimental to the major projects.

Um, you talk About the military Stuff. Yeah. So from an NZ dev perspective, um, we are, as I said in my open incomes, we are very aware that, um, there's a broad range of priorities across government and, you know, and money and resource is uh, tied. Um, so we've made prioritization decisions to manage our budget, but still allow us then the, uh, capacity to invest going forward.

Um, within the defense capability plan, there is investments for people, um, infrastructure and those other elements that combined to make a, a project into a military capability. Um, and from that, we've got an integrated investment plan going forward where we consider all of those aspects so that we can synchronize and deliver capabilities as promised nows from Tim Costley and from Tim. How, how Does that impact compare with, say the, the really high attrition we saw 21, 22, 23 people being taken away from these major projects to do things like, uh,

guarding MIQ facilities. And, and in your report here that we are looking at today, you talk about and to baseline twice, um, in AI network Army during the last, um, say six years before the election. H how, how does this compare with those challenges to Major? I'll make a couple of quick comments from an NZD perspective, then I'll hand over to Sarah.

Um, so our attrition rates have, um, you know, stabilized decline. Well, um, we still have a shortage of, uh, skilled people in the middle ranks, um, which is, uh, forcing us to, you know, really prioritize hard. We are looking at ways where we can utilize people with significant military experience to populate projects, allowing our younger personnel to focus on training and bringing, uh, new capabilities in into service, you know, via, you know, exercises and operations.

Sarah. Yeah, I'll just add that, um, while Matt's talked a lot about the military skill shortage, um, you will appreciate that IT specialists also very, um, well sought after in New Zealand. So network engineers, software engineers, cyber specialists. So one of the risks that we are focused on into the future with the defense capability plan is making sure that we can, um, recruit sufficient of these people without competing across government.

So we have to find a way to get these people in without, um, making it difficult for everyone else. The other comment is that the integrated project teams that we run, which are specialists, um, project management commercial and NCDF expertise are very small. We run very lean compared to other countries around the world. Um, so we are able to run these IPTs very successfully with small numbers of people.

Okay. Stuff on that. Um, you were, you were talking earlier around unmanned, uh, vessels, unmanned vehicles and stuff like that. Does that mean less jobs just to, and, and if so, is there a Yeah. What, what's, what's the strategy of it if it is? The general finding and finding within crewed systems is your workforce size doesn't necessarily change, but they change what they do.

So UNC crewed systems typically give you the ability to use a machine rather than a person somewhere where it's dirty, dangerous or boring, or you need just them to stay out there for a long time. But then you need a workforce in behind it to assess the information it's sending back, uh, to maintain control of it. Um, so you tend to see a workforce maybe shifts emphasis, but it doesn't necessarily reduce.

Okay. Start from Lauren. Oh, Um, thank you. So thinking about the staff and the workforce, and you talked about there are some forecasted potential gaps and shortages. Um, the private sector obviously pays a lot more for quite a lot of these, and you've got like, um, a DF doing a bit of a campaign to recruit some of our our team in that space.

Um, what is the plan, um, to retain the staff that you have currently with that capability and to also attract them over? And that possibly is probably more for you. That's, I think it's another NZD question, not, uh, aligned to our integrated investment plan. We also, um, uh, finalizing a workforce strategy that talks to, uh, attraction, it talks to retention, um, maybe a more agile, uh, system of, um, of, uh, managing skill.

Um, and then we're also working on, um, I guess I'd call it the supply side of that. So how the investments and people unfold so that we're, uh, developing and delivering people into our organization at the right time to actually utilize military capabilities. So looking forward, we're looking at a much more integrated, uh, suite of plans, which includes workforce. Thank you.

Um, just had a final question before we wrap up. Appreciate we're at time now, um, in relation to risk appetite, and we've seen obviously through this and previous reports that timeframes can be quite generous for different projects, and I understand that in the context of being in a relatively low risk, although threat environment I should say, and therefore the benefit of taking more time to get it right, being a higher priority.

Um, but in a more nuanced situation like we see now where there is a more, uh, or I guess a tighter timeframe around potentially delivering some of these capabilities, how do we manage that risk appetite around making decisions quickly versus potentially getting some wrong, but actually acting now to deliver capability?

And where are we on that spectrum? Oh, look, we've, um, both MOD and NCDF, uh, just adopted a, um, a framework I guess we, we call the minimum viable, uh, capability. So, um, you know, you talk how we used to trade schedule for cost and scope, um, now the minimum viable capability view is what can we get that's good enough, but fast within schedule.

So we're now trying to hold schedule constant, um, manage the cost, and if we have to scale back a little on scope, then we do so. Um, so we, we, we'll go for an 80% solution on time rather than 105% solution in five years, which is possibly something a lot of mil like militaries have been guilty of over recent times.
Okay. All right. Thank you very, very much for your time. Appreciate it.

We have Orion Health And As our first submitter for today.

Morning, Andrew. Is it? Yeah, that's right, please. Great to see. Cool. Good to be here. Excellent. All right. Thanks for your time this morning. And so the committee's been doing an ongoing piece of work in relation to the impact on non tariff barriers. Yeah. And we've been particularly interested in how that impacts the tech sector and uh, obviously from your unique perspective, we're keen to hear directly commercially how that impacts whether there are impacts in some of the areas that government may be able to look at adapting processes

to support, uh, industry growth. Yeah. Cool. So over to you, Andrew. Great. Well thank you, um, for the opportunity to appear today. Um, so I'm Andrew Bywater from, um, representing Orion Health. Um, so we're a homegrown digital health company, uh, with global Reach. Um, so we've been building software for the healthcare systems around the world, uh, for the last 30 years.

Um, we probably do come with a, a slightly different angle than what you might expect around, um, non tariff barriers, um, in particularly, um, the issues we see. Um, so we've been working from Auckland to Alberta to Albuquerque, um, but across 15 countries, the uk, Canada, us, uh, the Middle East, Saudi Arabia, Abu Dhabi, um, France, Spain.

So we do, we do the software for health systems across the world. Um, the New Zealand health tech sector, though, um, it's actually evolved, um, into quite a, a robust industry now. It's valued according to a report last year, about $2.1 billion. And a lot of that is Fisher and ourselves and admittedly, but there are about 200 companies, um, that work in medical devices, uh, digital health solutions, information technology.

So we're specifically a software company though. Um, and so there are a lot of other small companies though, with cool ideas being nurtured here. Um, but what we need is actually the home market, um, to be our launch pad for exports. And that is probably, um, what I'm gonna talk mostly about today. Um, so it's not so much about export wins, but what's holding that next generation of companies, um, from growing is actually, um, what's happening at home more than what we see happening here, more than necessarily overseas.

So if you see there's been a lot of attention around things like tariffs in the US or for software companies that, that hasn't affected us yet, um, I say yet. Um, but, and hopefully that remains the same. And when it comes to tax systems and that these, there's complexities, but it's fairly straightforward when, you know, we've been doing this for a long time.

Um, but as I say, uh, our biggest barrier that we've seen has actually been local procurement here in New Zealand. Um, so it's a bit of a shocking fact that we've found it easier to win business and the likes of Canada to the uk, Saudi Arabia, uh, than it is to get a pilot off the ground here, um, these days.

Um, and that is, while it sounds a little bit absurd, that is a bit of the reality that we face. So Orion Health started in the health sector, um, roughly about 25 years ago. Um, so it was already doing software, but, uh, found a niche there. Um, and it was a small $50,000 project in counties Manco. Um, and, and basically an idea and using an innovation fund there at counties Manco District Health Board.

Um, and we essentially grew by approving local results. We could deliver results locally and then, um, used that as a springboard. So, and used our New Zealand Inc. We get given a go around the world. Um, and having that credibility of those small projects here enabled us. That was the, the real thing we needed as a launchpad to secure investment and become attractive, um, to international markets.

Unfortunately, what we've seen now in the last few years is that's procurement here, particularly with government, has become actually the biggest domestic barrier to scale for software companies. Um, so without a strong home market, it's actually been much harder to grow offshore. Um, and we, we are confident enough, and we've been around long enough that we, we don't mind putting our heads up about the power pit to say this.

Um, but I know a lot of other small players in the New Zealand software sector do have found this. And this is not just in the last little while. This has been happening for 25 years now, 20 years. Um, and that, that's, um, an issue. So, um, it's much harder to grow offshore if you haven't got that strong base here.
